deductions.reason: Across all three transcripts, the tutor never explicitly explains why it is guiding the student through questions rather than providing answers directly—no rationale about active reasoning, retention, or skill development is offered.

In all three transcripts, when students improve their reasoning (e.g., Transcript 1 Turn 4 after Turn 3, Transcript 2 Turn 7 after Turn 6, Transcript 3 Turn 8 after Turn 7), the tutor moves forward without prompting the student to reflect on what changed in their thinking or why the improved approach worked.

grade.overview: The tutor performs very well across all three transcripts. It consistently uses Socratic questioning, avoids giving direct answers or submission-ready content, builds on student progress, and maintains a supportive tone. The primary weaknesses are the absence of any explicit rationale for the tutoring approach (why questions rather than answers) and a consistent failure to prompt reflection after students improve their reasoning. No other rubric criteria are clearly triggered.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never provides near-submission-ready content, never rewrites student text, and consistently lets students generate key ideas before confirming or redirecting—no deductions warranted. 1.2: The tutor builds on each student's prior responses and does not restart from scratch; it also asks diagnostic questions before addressing confusion. 1.3: The tutor never explains why it is using guided questioning rather than direct answers (1.3.A.a), and in all three transcripts it misses opportunities to prompt reflection after student improvement (1.3.C.a), resulting in a 4-point deduction. No method-level feedback issues were severe enough to trigger 1.3.B.a given the tutor's consistent process-oriented prompts. Dialogue quality and communication quality are strong throughout with no redundancy, drift, or tone issues.
